---
title: "The Checklist, Assessment 8, and the Capstone"
date: "2026-08-10"
---

# The Checklist, Assessment 8, and the Capstone {.title}

::: notes
Welcome back to Week Eight, Lab Eight, part ten of ten — the final segment of the lab and the camp. In part nine we prepared for the questions and the fatal-versus-survivable triage. Now we close: the submission checklist a reviewer would run, how this lab maps onto Assessment Eight, and the single sentence the eight weeks existed to let you say.
:::

## The Checklist a Reviewer Runs

- Poster PDF compiles clean — title, `\input`ed abstract, six panels in order
- Abstract generated, **200–300 words**, numbers match the headline
- No hard-coded results: `grep -E "[0-9]\.[0-9]" paper/poster.tex` finds only the year
- QR resolves to the **public** repo — scanned before the symposium
- One-click rebuild and honesty test pass; `SEED` fixed; no secrets

::: notes
Check a box only when a file or command output proves it. The poster PDF compiles clean, with the title block, the input-ed abstract, and the six panels in reading order. The abstract is regenerated by your analysis script, two hundred to three hundred words, and every number matches the headline because both come from the same variables. No hard-coded results: grep poster-dot-tex for a digit-dot-digit pattern and you find only the year and panel sizes, never a coefficient. The QR resolves to the public repo — scanned before the symposium, not a private repo and not a four-oh-four. The one-click rebuild runs pull, build, analysis, abstract, poster on a fresh clone, and the honesty test shows the artifacts byte-identical, figure timestamp pinned. The seed is fixed in config. And git-ls-files shows no dot-env, no key files, no parquet, no CRSP or Compustat.
:::

## Talk, Questions, Submission

- Talk fits **seven minutes**; cut line on a sticky note
- Each threats-table row has a four-part answer, tagged fatal or survivable
- Your **retreat position** for the worst critique is written out
- Submission = poster PDF + abstract + repo URL + QR
- The repo **is** the submission — everything else points back into it

::: notes
The last boxes are the talk and the handover. The talk fits in seven minutes, rehearsed at least three times, the cut line pre-decided on a sticky note — or pinned in the breakout chat for remote interns. Question prep is done: each threats-table row has a four-part answer, tagged fatal or survivable, and your retreat position for the worst critique is written out in full. The submission is assembled: poster PDF, abstract, repo URL with a working QR code, and the packet verified by a fresh-clone make-all. Internalize this: the repository is the submission. There is no separate upload. What you push is graded, the QR resolves to that repo, and the commit history — plan tagged before results, deviations logged after — is part of the grade.
:::

## Assessment 8 and the Capstone Gallery

- Assessment 8 *is* the capstone — same 200-point rubric as Assessment 7
- A7 graded the design half; A8 grades the execution half
- Audit: does `make all` on a fresh clone reproduce headline *and* abstract?
- Read the **capstone gallery** — find its seams, run `make all`
- Optional companion paper: same generated abstract, an `article` class

::: notes
This lab is the build bench; Assessment Eight is the capstone. Assessment Seven graded the design half; Assessment Eight grades the execution half on that same two-hundred-point rubric — the analysis, the robustness battery, the poster and abstract, the peer review, and the two surfaces this lab built. So treat the checklist as a pre-grade audit: does make-all on a fresh clone reproduce your headline number and abstract? Then read the capstone gallery — five finished exemplars, each with a talk and a packet. Read one not for its result but for its seams: find its identification section, the residual its threats table admits, run its make-all, imagine its six poster panels. That translation is the work of this lab. And for a journal-shaped artifact, the optional companion appendix preserves the full LaTeX manuscript — same generated abstract, an article class instead of a poster.
:::

## Key Takeaway {.title}

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
You started ASSIP computing an 8% average return and thinking it was an answer — it was the first question. Now you have original research, packaged so a skeptic can rebuild every claim with one command. Compile the poster, finish the packet, rehearse the defense — then go say it.
:::

::: notes
To close the lab and the camp: you started ASSIP computing that the average return was eight percent and thinking it was an answer. It was the first question. What you have now — a question nobody had cleanly answered, attacked with a design you can defend sentence by sentence, estimated with honest uncertainty, robust to the choices you worried about, distilled onto a poster panel a stranger believes in ninety seconds, and packaged so a skeptic rebuilds every claim with one command — is original empirical research. The single sentence the eight weeks existed to let you say truthfully, beside your poster on August twelfth: here is what I found, here is why you should believe it, and here is the QR code that lets you check me. Compile the poster, finish the packet, rehearse the defense. Then say it.
:::
